planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; white butterfly ginger is one of the most powerfully fragrant flowering plants cultivated in warm climates — the pure white, butterfly-shaped flowers releasing an intense, sweet, exotic fragrance that carries a considerable distance in evening air. It is the national flower of Cuba (known as Mariposa) and has naturalized throughout the tropical world. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a reliable perennial, dying back in winter and resprouting vigorously in spring. Thrives in moist, boggy conditions; can grow at pond margins.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, humus-rich, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; white butterfly ginger is regarded by garden enthusiasts in warm climates as one of the most intensely fragrant of all flowering plants — a native of the Himalayan foothills of India and Nepal (naturalized throughout tropical Asia, the Caribbean, and subtropical America) that produces in summer and autumn large, terminal spikes of pure-white, butterfly-shaped flowers with an extraordinarily powerful, sweet, exotic, slightly jasmine-like fragrance of unusual complexity and intensity that fills the garden air, particularly in the warm evenings when the fragrance intensifies and carries for considerable distances. The individual flowers are the distinctive hedychium form: a long, narrow, trumpet tube opening to three spreading, pure-white upper petals (the 'butterfly' shape) with a central, broad, pure-white labellum and two narrow, lateral staminodes creating the characteristic butterfly appearance. They are produced in succession on terminal spikes held above the long, lance-shaped, bright-green, ginger-family foliage for weeks in summer and fall. In zone 9 the rhizomes survive winter underground and resprout with considerable vigor each spring, producing clumps of erect, 5–7-foot stems by midsummer. Cuba named it the national flower under the name 'Mariposa' (butterfly). The national flower of Cuba and one of the quintessential plants of tropical and subtropical garden traditions worldwide.

subcategory: classic culinary and medicinal spice rhizome

planting.note: Plant fresh, plump ginger rhizome sections (each with at least one eye/bud) in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; culinary ginger is not fully sun-tolerant in hot climates — it performs best in morning sun with afternoon shade or dappled filtered light. Plant rhizome sections 2–4 inches deep and 8–12 inches apart. Harvest young ('green') ginger 4–5 months after planting or mature ginger after leaves die back (8–10 months). In zone 9 it may remain in the ground year-round as a perennial. Use supermarket ginger or specialist growers for seed rhizomes.

directions: Plant fresh, plump rhizome sections with viable buds in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade, keeping the rhizome section just below soil surface level; culinary ginger is the universal spice plant — one of humanity's most ancient, most widely used, and most globally traded spice and medicinal plants, native to tropical Asia (probably India or coastal southern China) but cultivated throughout the tropics for at least 3,000 years and now grown in tropical and subtropical regions of every continent. The rhizome (underground stem) is the harvested, used portion: the knobby, multi-branched, pale-tan to buff-tan fresh rhizome with its characteristic pungent-hot, warm-spicy, aromatic flavor and scent comes from the volatile oil compounds (primarily gingerol, shogaol, and zingerone) that also provide the medicinal properties (anti-nausea, anti-inflammatory, digestive). In the garden, it is a handsome, substantial plant reaching 3–4 feet — erect, reed-like stems with alternating, glossy, lance-shaped, aromatic leaves of deep green — with an attractively tropical, lush appearance. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it grows as a reliable perennial, the aerial stems dying back in winter and rhizomes resprouting each spring. Young (green) ginger can be harvested after 4–5 months (mild, juicy, less fiber); mature ginger after the leaves die back naturally (8–10 months, full pungency). One of the most rewarding home-garden edibles for partial-shade plots in zones 8–11.

subcategory: golden-rhizomed anti-inflammatory spice and dye plant

planting.note: Plant fresh turmeric rhizome sections (each with at least one growing point) 2 inches deep and 12 inches apart in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; turmeric grows and produces best with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot zone 9 climates — harsh midday sun in summer heat can stress the foliage. Harvest after leaves die back in fall (8–10 months from planting). The bright orange-yellow flesh of the rhizome stains skin, clothing, and surfaces permanently — handle with care. In zone 9 rhizomes may overwinter in the ground.

directions: Plant fresh rhizome sections with viable growing points 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; turmeric is the ancient golden spice of South Asian cooking and traditional medicine — the underground rhizome that when harvested, dried, and ground yields the vivid, saturated, warm-golden-yellow powder that gives curry its characteristic color, imparts a mild, earthy-peppery-slightly-bitter flavor, and delivers the curcumin compound that has become one of the most researched natural anti-inflammatory substances in modern phytomedicine. Native to tropical South Asia (most likely India or Southeast Asia), it has been cultivated throughout the tropical world for at least 4,000 years as both spice and dye (it provided the yellow color in Asian textiles long before synthetic dyes). The garden plant itself is a handsome, lush, tropical-looking Zingiberaceae (ginger family) member with large, paddle-shaped, bright-green, distinctly ribbed and veined leaves similar to those of cannas or gingers, reaching 3–4 feet in height and providing excellent tropical effect in the part-shade garden throughout the growing season. The rhizomes are internally a vivid, saturated orange-yellow (the curcumin pigment) and stain anything they contact that same bright orange-yellow. Harvest the fresh rhizomes in fall after the aerial stems die back; they can be used fresh (milder, more aromatic), dried and ground (the familiar powder), or replanted for next season. A highly rewarding dual-purpose ornamental and culinary/medicinal herb for zone 8–11 shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; greater galangal is a large, robust ginger relative from coastal Southeast Asia that thrives in zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens as a clumping perennial, reaching 4–8 feet with large, handsome, lance-shaped leaves. The white-striped-red rhizome is harvested year-round once established and used fresh in Thai curries, soups (tom kha), and marinades. It has a distinctive flavor quite different from culinary ginger — more piney, citrusy, medicinal, and peppery. Tender; damaged below 28°F.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; greater galangal is the essential culinary rhizome of Southeast Asian cooking — a large, handsome ginger-family plant native to coastal Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand) that provides the distinctive aromatic flavoring in Thai tom kha soup, Thai curry pastes, Indonesian rendang, Cambodian dishes, and numerous other Southeast Asian preparations where it is as fundamental an ingredient as garlic or lemongrass. The flavor of galangal rhizome is completely distinct from culinary ginger (Zingiber officinale): sharper, more medicinal and piney, with strong citrus-pepper notes and a resinous, camphor-like quality that is immediately recognizable in the cuisines that use it — the characteristic flavor that makes tom kha soup taste like tom kha and nothing else. The rhizome is distinguished visually from ginger by its dense, concentric-ringed, smooth skin with distinctive rose-pink to red-tinged growing points (unlike the warty tan skin of ginger). In the garden it is a very handsome, bold, tropical plant — a large, upright clump of robust stems with broad, dark-green, somewhat glossy, lance-shaped leaves reaching 4–8 feet, with attractive white-and-maroon-striped flowers occasionally in summer. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it grows as a reliable perennial in sheltered positions with consistent moisture. Once established, rhizomes can be harvested year-round as needed by carefully digging at the clump margin.
